solution:
Everyone (presumably) knows that the set of solutions of a system of
linear first-order differential equations with constant coefficients
is $n$-dimensional, with basis vectors of the form $f_{i}(t)
\vec{v}_{i}$ (i.e.\ a function times a constant vector), where the
$\vec{v}_{i}$ are linearly independent. In
particular, our solution $\vec{x}(t)$ can be written as $\sum_{i=1}^{n}
c_{i}f_{i}(t) \vec{v}_{1}$.

Choose a vector $\vec{w}$ orthogonal to $\vec{v}_{2}, \dots,
\vec{v}_{n}$ but not to $\vec{v}_1$. Since $\vec{x}(t) \to 0$ as $t
\to \infty$, the same is true of $\vec{w} \cdot \vec{x}$; but that is
simply $(\vec{w} \cdot \vec{v}_{1}) c_{1} f_{1}(t)$. In other words,
if $c_{i} \neq 0$, then $f_{i}(t)$ must also go to 0.

However, it is easy to exhibit a solution which does not go to 0. The
sum of the eigenvalues of the matrix $A = (a_{ij})$, also known as the
trace of $A$, being the sum of the diagonal entries of $A$, is
nonnegative, so $A$ has an eigenvalue $\lambda$ with nonnegative real
part, and a corresponding eigenvector $\vec{v}$. Then $e^{\lambda t}
\vec{v}$ is a solution that does not go to 0. (If $\lambda$ is not
real, add this solution to its complex conjugate to get a real
solution, which still doesn't go to 0.)

Hence one of the $c_{i}$, say $c_{1}$, is zero, in which case
$\vec{x}(t) \cdot \vec{w} = 0$ for all $t$.

descriptive_long.question:
(real-valued) functions of a single variable $funcvar$ which satisfy
\begin{align*}
\frac{d firstvar}{d timevar} &= coefoneone firstvar + coefonetwo secondvar + \cdots + coefonelast lastxvar \\
\frac{d secondvar}{d timevar} &= coeftwoone firstvar + coeftwotwo secondvar + \cdots + coeftwolast lastxvar \\
\vdots && \vdots \\
\frac{d lastxvar}{d timevar} &= coeflastone firstvar + a_{n2} secondvar + \cdots + coeflastlast lastxvar
\end{align*}
for some constants $coefgenij>0$. Suppose that for all $indexi$, $xindexvar(timevar) \to 0$ as $timevar \to \infty$. Are the functions $firstvar, secondvar,\dots,lastxvar$ necessarily linearly dependent?
descriptive_long.solution:
Everyone (presumably) knows that the set of solutions of a system of linear first-order differential equations with constant coefficients is $dimension$-dimensional, with basis vectors of the form $funcindex(timevar) \vec{vectorindex}$ (i.e.\ a function times a constant vector), where the $\vec{vectorindex}$ are linearly independent. In particular, our solution $\vec{x}(timevar)$ can be written as $\sum_{indexi=1}^{dimension} constindex funcindex(timevar) \vec{vectorone}$.

Choose a vector $\vec{vectorw}$ orthogonal to $\vec{v}_{2}, \dots, \vec{v}_{dimension}$ but not to $\vec{vectorone}$. Since $\vec{x}(timevar) \to 0$ as $timevar \to \infty$, the same is true of $\vec{vectorw} \cdot \vec{x}$; but that is simply $(\vec{vectorw} \cdot \vec{vectorone}) constone funcindex(timevar)$. In other words, if $constindex \neq 0$, then $funcindex(timevar)$ must also go to 0.

However, it is easy to exhibit a solution which does not go to 0. The sum of the eigenvalues of the matrix $matrixa = (coefgenij)$, also known as the trace of $matrixa$, being the sum of the diagonal entries of $matrixa$, is nonnegative, so $matrixa$ has an eigenvalue $eigenlambda$ with nonnegative real part, and a corresponding eigenvector $\vec{v}$. Then $e^{eigenlambda timevar} \vec{v}$ is a solution that does not go to 0. (If $eigenlambda$ is not real, add this solution to its complex conjugate to get a real solution, which still doesn't go to 0.)

Hence one of the $constindex$, say $constone$, is zero, in which case $\vec{x}(timevar) \cdot \vec{vectorw} = 0$ for all $timevar$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
(real-valued) functions of a single variable $ploughman$ which satisfy
\begin{align*}
\frac{dmarigolds}{dpaperclip} &= raincloud marigolds + peppermint pinecones + \cdots +
toothpick lampstand \\
\frac{dpinecones}{dpaperclip} &= binoculars marigolds + beachball pinecones + \cdots +
gooseberry lampstand \\
\vdots && \vdots \\
\frac{dlampstand}{dpaperclip} &= firebrick marigolds + a_{n2} pinecones + \cdots +
thumbtack lampstand
\end{align*}
for some constants $strawberries>0$. Suppose that for all $paintwork$, $drumstick(paperclip)
\to 0$ as $paperclip \to \infty$. Are the functions $marigolds,pinecones,\dots,lampstand$
necessarily linearly dependent?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Everyone (presumably) knows that the set of solutions of a system of
linear first-order differential equations with constant coefficients
is $shoelaces$-dimensional, with basis vectors of the form $sugarcane(paperclip)
\vec{candlestick}$ (i.e.\ a function times a constant vector), where the
$\vec{candlestick}$ are linearly independent. In
particular, our solution $\vec{x}(paperclip)$ can be written as $\sum_{paintwork=1}^{shoelaces}
snowflake sugarcane(paperclip) \vec{goldfinch}$.

Choose a vector $\vec{hairbrush}$ orthogonal to $\vec{v}_{2}, \dots,
\vec{v}_{n}$ but not to $\vec{goldfinch}$. Since $\vec{x}(paperclip) \to 0$ as $paperclip
\to \infty$, the same is true of $\vec{hairbrush} \cdot \vec{x}$; but that is
simply $(\vec{hairbrush} \cdot \vec{goldfinch}) honeycomb ploughman_{1}(paperclip)$. In other words,
if $snowflake \neq 0$, then $sugarcane(paperclip)$ must also go to 0.

However, it is easy to exhibit a solution which does not go to 0. The
sum of the eigenvalues of the matrix $masterplan = (strawberries)$, also known as the
trace of $masterplan$, being the sum of the diagonal entries of $masterplan$, is
nonnegative, so $masterplan$ has an eigenvalue $buttercup$ with nonnegative real
part, and a corresponding eigenvector $\vec{v}$. Then $e^{buttercup paperclip}
\vec{v}$ is a solution that does not go to 0. (If $buttercup$ is not
real, add this solution to its complex conjugate to get a real
solution, which still doesn't go to 0.)

Hence one of the $snowflake$, say $honeycomb$, is zero, in which case
$\vec{x}(paperclip) \cdot \vec{hairbrush} = 0$ for all $paperclip$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
(real-valued) functions of a single variable fixedfunc which satisfy
\begin{align*}
\frac{dconstantone}{dtimeless} &= negativeone constantone + negativetwo constanttwo + \cdots +
negativeend constantend \\
\frac{dconstanttwo}{dtimeless} &= negativealpha constantone + negativebeta constanttwo + \cdots +
negativegamma constantend \\
\vdots && \vdots \\
\frac{dconstantend}{dtimeless} &= negativedelta constantone + a_{n2} constanttwo + \cdots +
negativetheta constantend
\end{align*}
for some constants negativepair>0. Suppose that for all outsider, constantindex(timeless)
\to 0 as timeless \to \infty. Are the functions constantone, constanttwo, \dots, constantend
necessarily linearly dependent?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Everyone (presumably) knows that the set of solutions of a system of
linear first-order differential equations with constant coefficients
is singularnum-dimensional, with basis vectors of the form fixedindex(timeless)
\vec{scalarindex} (i.e.\ a function times a constant vector), where the
\vec{scalarindex} are linearly independent. In
particular, our solution \vec{x}(timeless) can be written as \sum_{outsider=1}^{singularnum}
 nullindex fixedindex(timeless) \vec{scalarsingle}.

Choose a vector \vec{emptiness} orthogonal to \vec{v}_{2}, \dots,
\vec{v}_{n} but not to \vec{scalarsingle}. Since \vec{x}(timeless) \to 0 as timeless
\to \infty, the same is true of \vec{emptiness} \cdot \vec{x}; but that is
simply (\vec{emptiness} \cdot \vec{scalarsingle}) nullsingle fixedfunc(timeless). In other words,
if nullindex \neq 0, then fixedindex(timeless) must also go to 0.

However, it is easy to exhibit a solution which does not go to 0. The
sum of the eigenvalues of the matrix voidmatrix = (negativepair), also known as the
trace of voidmatrix, being the sum of the diagonal entries of voidmatrix, is
nonnegative, so voidmatrix has an eigenvalue positiveroot with nonnegative real
part, and a corresponding eigenvector \vec{v}. Then e^{positiveroot timeless}
\vec{v} is a solution that does not go to 0. (If positiveroot is not
real, add this solution to its complex conjugate to get a real
solution, which still doesn't go to 0.)

Hence one of the nullindex, say nullsingle, is zero, in which case
\vec{x}(timeless) \cdot \vec{emptiness} = 0 for all timeless.

garbled_string.question:
(real-valued) functions of a single variable $sdqwejkl$ which satisfy
\begin{align*}
\frac{d qzxwvtnp}{d zxcvbnma} &= lkjhgfds qzxwvtnp + poiuytre hjgrksla + \cdots +
qazwsxed mbcxdfeo \\
\frac{d hjgrksla}{d zxcvbnma} &= edcrfvtg qzxwvtnp + yhnujmki hjgrksla + \cdots +
ujmnhygt mbcxdfeo \\
\vdots && \vdots \\
\frac{d mbcxdfeo}{d zxcvbnma} &= ikmjuynh qzxwvtnp + a_{n2} hjgrksla + \cdots +
mnbvcxzq mbcxdfeo
\end{align*}
for some constants $rfvgytbh>0$. Suppose that for all $lpoikmnj$, $klmnrsop(zxcvbnma)
\to 0$ as $zxcvbnma \to \infty$. Are the functions $qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,\dots,mbcxdfeo$
necessarily linearly dependent?
garbled_string.solution:
Everyone (presumably) knows that the set of solutions of a system of
linear first-order differential equations with constant coefficients
is $oikjuhtg$-dimensional, with basis vectors of the form $plokmjnh(zxcvbnma)
\vec{cdewsxza}$ (i.e.\ a function times a constant vector), where the
$\vec{cdewsxza}$ are linearly independent. In
particular, our solution $\vec{x}(zxcvbnma)$ can be written as $\sum_{lpoikmnj=1}^{oikjuhtg}
tgbyhnuj plokmjnh(zxcvbnma) \vec{plmkoijn}$.

Choose a vector $\vec{njuhtgfr}$ orthogonal to $\vec{cdewsxza}, \dots,
\vec{v}_{n}$ but not to $\vec{plmkoijn}$. Since $\vec{x}(zxcvbnma) \to 0$ as $zxcvbnma
\to \infty$, the same is true of $\vec{njuhtgfr} \cdot \vec{x}$; but that is
simply $(\vec{njuhtgfr} \cdot \vec{plmkoijn}) vfrtgbhy plokmjnh(zxcvbnma)$. In other words,
if $tgbyhnuj \neq 0$, then $plokmjnh(zxcvbnma)$ must also go to 0.

However, it is easy to exhibit a solution which does not go to 0. The
sum of the eigenvalues of the matrix $bgtfrved = (rfvgytbh)$, also known as the
trace of $bgtfrved$, being the sum of the diagonal entries of $bgtfrved$, is
nonnegative, so $bgtfrved$ has an eigenvalue $asdfghjk$ with nonnegative real
part, and a corresponding eigenvector $\vec{v}$. Then $e^{asdfghjk zxcvbnma}
\vec{v}$ is a solution that does not go to 0. (If $asdfghjk$ is not
real, add this solution to its complex conjugate to get a real
solution, which still doesn't go to 0.)

Hence one of the $tgbyhnuj$, say $vfrtgbhy$, is zero, in which case
$\vec{x}(zxcvbnma) \cdot \vec{njuhtgfr} = 0$ for all $zxcvbnma$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and let  
  A : \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow  M_n(\mathbb{C})  
be a 2\pi -periodic C^1 matrix-valued map such that every pair of matrices of the
family commutes,  
  A(s)A(t) = A(t)A(s)  for all s , t \in  \mathbb{R}.  (*)

Denote by \Phi (t) the principal fundamental matrix of the linear system  

  x'(t) = A(t) x(t), t \geq  0,  x(0)=x_0 \in  \mathbb{C}^n,  

normalised by \Phi (0)=I_n, and put  

  M := \Phi (2\pi )                             (monodromy matrix).

Assume that the spectral radius of M is strictly larger than one,  

  \rho (M) = max{|\mu | : \mu  \in  \sigma (M)} > 1.  (1)

Let x : [0,\infty ) \longrightarrow  \mathbb{C}^n be any C^1-solution whose coordinates all decay,  

  lim_{t\to \infty } x_i(t) = 0  (i = 1,\ldots ,n).  (2)

Prove that the coordinate functions x_1,\ldots ,x_n are linearly dependent over \mathbb{C}; i.e. show that there exist complex constants c_1,\ldots ,c_n, not all zero, such that  

  c_1 x_1(t) + \cdots  + c_n x_n(t) \equiv  0  for every t \geq  0.

(The commutativity hypothesis (*) is automatic, for example, when A(t) is diagonal for every t, or when A(t)=f(t)B with a fixed matrix B and a scalar
function f.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0 - Consequences of the commutativity assumption.  
Because all A(t) commute, so do their integrals; hence

  \Phi (t)=exp (\int _0^t A(\tau )d\tau )  for all t\in \mathbb{R},             (3)

and \Phi (t) commutes with \Phi (s) for every s,t.  In particular

  \Phi (t) M = M \Phi (t)  for all t.                     (4)

Put  

  B := (1/2\pi ) \int _0^{2\pi } A(\tau )d\tau .  

Owing to (3) we have  

  M = \Phi (2\pi ) = exp(2\pi  B).                           (5)

Step 1 - Unstable Floquet multiplier.  
From (1) choose \mu \in \sigma (M) with |\mu |>1 and fix a non-zero left eigenvector

  w* M = \mu  w*.                                     (6)

Write w for the column-conjugate transpose of w*, i.e. regard w as a constant
row vector.

Step 2 - A functional equation.  
For the given decaying solution set  

  f(t):= w\cdot x(t)=w* x(t).                           (7)

Because x(t)=\Phi (t)c with c:=x_0, we obtain, using (4) and (6),

  f(t+2\pi ) = w* \Phi (t+2\pi )c
           = w* \Phi (t)M c            (by (4))
           = \mu  w* \Phi (t)c
           = \mu  f(t).                                (8)

Thus f satisfies the linear functional equation

  f(t+2\pi )=\mu  f(t).                                 (9)

Step 3 - Decay forces f\equiv 0.  
Assume, for a contradiction, that f is not identically zero; choose t_0\geq 0 with
f(t_0)\neq 0.  Iterating (9) gives

  f(t_0+2\pi k)=\mu ^{\,k}f(t_0), k=0,1,2,\ldots .              (10)

Taking absolute values and using |\mu |>1 we find |f(t_0+2\pi k)|\to \infty  as k\to \infty ,
contradicting the fact that each coordinate of x - and hence every constant
linear combination of them - tends to 0 by (2).  Therefore

  f(t)\equiv 0  for all t\geq 0.                            (11)

Step 4 - Linear dependence of the coordinates.  
Write w=(c_1,\ldots ,c_n).  Then (11) is precisely

  c_1 x_1(t)+\cdots +c_n x_n(t) \equiv  0  (t\geq 0).               (12)

Because w* is a non-zero left eigenvector, the vector  
(c_1,\ldots ,c_n) is not the zero vector, so the coordinate functions are linearly
dependent, completing the proof. \blacksquare 

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Non-autonomous system: The coefficient matrix now depends on t, so
   simple eigen-decomposition of a constant matrix no longer works.
   One must invoke Floquet theory and handle a periodic fundamental
   matrix decomposition.
2. Spectral condition on the monodromy matrix replaces “trace > 0” and
   requires understanding of Floquet multipliers and exponents—concepts
   absent from the original exercise.
3. The proof uses left eigenvectors of the monodromy matrix and a
   functional equation f(t+T)=μ f(t) whose analysis is more delicate
   than the exponential estimate for constant-coefficient systems.
4. Because of the periodic factor P(t) in Φ(t), the naive attempt to
   copy the original argument fails; a new idea (working with the
   monodromy matrix and its eigenvectors) is essential.
5. The learner must combine several advanced tools—Floquet theory,
   spectral radius considerations, and functional iteration—to obtain
   the desired linear dependence.  These additional layers of theory
   and the subtler logical steps make the enhanced variant
   significantly harder than both the original problem and the current
   kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and let  
  A : \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow  M_n(\mathbb{C})  
be a 2\pi -periodic C^1 matrix-valued map such that every pair of matrices of the
family commutes,  
  A(s)A(t) = A(t)A(s)  for all s , t \in  \mathbb{R}.  (*)

Denote by \Phi (t) the principal fundamental matrix of the linear system  

  x'(t) = A(t) x(t), t \geq  0,  x(0)=x_0 \in  \mathbb{C}^n,  

normalised by \Phi (0)=I_n, and put  

  M := \Phi (2\pi )                             (monodromy matrix).

Assume that the spectral radius of M is strictly larger than one,  

  \rho (M) = max{|\mu | : \mu  \in  \sigma (M)} > 1.  (1)

Let x : [0,\infty ) \longrightarrow  \mathbb{C}^n be any C^1-solution whose coordinates all decay,  

  lim_{t\to \infty } x_i(t) = 0  (i = 1,\ldots ,n).  (2)

Prove that the coordinate functions x_1,\ldots ,x_n are linearly dependent over \mathbb{C}; i.e. show that there exist complex constants c_1,\ldots ,c_n, not all zero, such that  

  c_1 x_1(t) + \cdots  + c_n x_n(t) \equiv  0  for every t \geq  0.

(The commutativity hypothesis (*) is automatic, for example, when A(t) is diagonal for every t, or when A(t)=f(t)B with a fixed matrix B and a scalar
function f.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0 - Consequences of the commutativity assumption.  
Because all A(t) commute, so do their integrals; hence

  \Phi (t)=exp (\int _0^t A(\tau )d\tau )  for all t\in \mathbb{R},             (3)

and \Phi (t) commutes with \Phi (s) for every s,t.  In particular

  \Phi (t) M = M \Phi (t)  for all t.                     (4)

Put  

  B := (1/2\pi ) \int _0^{2\pi } A(\tau )d\tau .  

Owing to (3) we have  

  M = \Phi (2\pi ) = exp(2\pi  B).                           (5)

Step 1 - Unstable Floquet multiplier.  
From (1) choose \mu \in \sigma (M) with |\mu |>1 and fix a non-zero left eigenvector

  w* M = \mu  w*.                                     (6)

Write w for the column-conjugate transpose of w*, i.e. regard w as a constant
row vector.

Step 2 - A functional equation.  
For the given decaying solution set  

  f(t):= w\cdot x(t)=w* x(t).                           (7)

Because x(t)=\Phi (t)c with c:=x_0, we obtain, using (4) and (6),

  f(t+2\pi ) = w* \Phi (t+2\pi )c
           = w* \Phi (t)M c            (by (4))
           = \mu  w* \Phi (t)c
           = \mu  f(t).                                (8)

Thus f satisfies the linear functional equation

  f(t+2\pi )=\mu  f(t).                                 (9)

Step 3 - Decay forces f\equiv 0.  
Assume, for a contradiction, that f is not identically zero; choose t_0\geq 0 with
f(t_0)\neq 0.  Iterating (9) gives

  f(t_0+2\pi k)=\mu ^{\,k}f(t_0), k=0,1,2,\ldots .              (10)

Taking absolute values and using |\mu |>1 we find |f(t_0+2\pi k)|\to \infty  as k\to \infty ,
contradicting the fact that each coordinate of x - and hence every constant
linear combination of them - tends to 0 by (2).  Therefore

  f(t)\equiv 0  for all t\geq 0.                            (11)

Step 4 - Linear dependence of the coordinates.  
Write w=(c_1,\ldots ,c_n).  Then (11) is precisely

  c_1 x_1(t)+\cdots +c_n x_n(t) \equiv  0  (t\geq 0).               (12)

Because w* is a non-zero left eigenvector, the vector  
(c_1,\ldots ,c_n) is not the zero vector, so the coordinate functions are linearly
dependent, completing the proof. \blacksquare 

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Non-autonomous system: The coefficient matrix now depends on t, so
   simple eigen-decomposition of a constant matrix no longer works.
   One must invoke Floquet theory and handle a periodic fundamental
   matrix decomposition.
2. Spectral condition on the monodromy matrix replaces “trace > 0” and
   requires understanding of Floquet multipliers and exponents—concepts
   absent from the original exercise.
3. The proof uses left eigenvectors of the monodromy matrix and a
   functional equation f(t+T)=μ f(t) whose analysis is more delicate
   than the exponential estimate for constant-coefficient systems.
4. Because of the periodic factor P(t) in Φ(t), the naive attempt to
   copy the original argument fails; a new idea (working with the
   monodromy matrix and its eigenvectors) is essential.
5. The learner must combine several advanced tools—Floquet theory,
   spectral radius considerations, and functional iteration—to obtain
   the desired linear dependence.  These additional layers of theory
   and the subtler logical steps make the enhanced variant
   significantly harder than both the original problem and the current
